frontmatter.title: The category of lift-algebras is cocomplete

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos is cocomplete.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows because \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } under connected colimits and coproducts

mainmatter.p: We will deduce the closure of \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } under connected colimits using general results about monads. Then we use the results of Linton to deduce the cocompleteness of \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} }.
For any dcpo A, the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \mathsf{L} takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix another lax square of the following form:
We must show that there exists a unique morphism \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D factoring the square above through { \left \{ d_ \bot \sqsubseteq d_A \right \} } as follows:

We will define \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D to take a ^{ ? } : \mathsf{L} {A} to the least upper bound of the directed subset U = { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_A a \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \subseteq D. As this assignment evidently has the correct factorization property, it remains just to check that it is continuous. Letting V be a directed subset of \mathsf{L} {A}, we compute:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} d \bigsqcup V & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid \bigsqcup V = \eta _A a \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid \eta _Aa \in V \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup { \left \{ \bigsqcup { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } \in V \right \} } \\ & = \bigsqcup { \left \{ da ^{ ? } \mid a ^{ ? } \in V \right \} } \end{aligned}
block.p: For uniqueness, suppose that we have two morphisms \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d,d'} D factoring our square in the sense described above and fix a ^{ ? } : \mathsf{L} {A}:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} d a ^{ ? } & = d \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d \, { \left ( \eta _Aa \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d' \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d' \, { \left ( \eta _Aa \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = d' \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = d'a ^{ ? } \end{aligned}

mainmatter.p: Let A_ \bullet be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \mathcal{I}. Then the lifting endofunctor \mathsf{L} on dcpos preserves the colimit of A_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let A_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { \left \{ A_ \infty \right \} } be a universal cocone; we must show that \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { \left \{ D \right \} } factors uniquely through \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} D to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define { \mathbf{1} _{ } } \xrightarrow{h_ \bot} { D } to be the element d_i \bot determined by an arbitrary object i \in \mathcal{I}. To see that this is well-defined, fix i,j \in \mathcal{I} to check that d_i \bot =d_j \bot. As \mathcal{I} is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag i \leadsto j \in \mathcal{I}; ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j in \mathcal{I}, we have d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot. This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone d_ \bullet.
Next we define { A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{h _{ A_ \infty }} { D } using the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }:

To arrange h _{ A_ \infty } and h_ \bot into the desired lax square, we must check that for all a_ \infty :A_ \infty we have h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } a_ \infty. Fixing arbitrary i \in \mathcal{I} and a \in A_i, it suffices to check that h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _{ i } a. As h_ \bot =d_i \bot and h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _ia = d_i \eta _{ A_i } a, our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of { \mathsf{L} {A_i} } \xrightarrow{d_i} { D }.
Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{h} { D } factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty }:

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} { D } corresponding to the cocone { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } to be the morphism h defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \mathsf{L} {A_i}, it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that h \bot = h_ \bot.
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{k,k'} { D } factoring { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } through { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } } are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } once more to reduce this to checking that k,k' agree on \bot and on \eta _{ A_ \bullet }. The former follows directly from the strictness of \mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }, as we have k \bot = k { \left ( \mathsf{L} \iota _ \bullet \bot \right )} =d_ \bullet \bot. For the latter, we must check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } =k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty }; by the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }, it suffices to check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet. By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to d_ \bullet \circ \eta _{ A_ \bullet }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} }} { \mathsf{dcpo} }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
The forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} }} { \mathsf{dcpo} } creates whatever colimits \mathsf{L} preserves.

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos has all small coproducts.
This is the first part of Corollary 2 of Linton's Coequalizers in categories of algebras.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The coproduct of lift-algebras is computed as a reflexive coequalizer, whose existence we have already established. Given an index set I and a diagram { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } }, the colimit of the following reflexive pair is the coproduct of { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } as shown by Linton:

frontmatter.title: The category of lift-algebras is cocomplete

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos is cocomplete.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows because \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } under connected colimits and coproducts

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos has all small coproducts.
This is the first part of Corollary 2 of Linton's Coequalizers in categories of algebras.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The coproduct of lift-algebras is computed as a reflexive coequalizer, whose existence we have already established. Given an index set I and a diagram { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } }, the colimit of the following reflexive pair is the coproduct of { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } as shown by Linton:

mainmatter.p: Let A_ \bullet be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \mathcal{I}. Then the lifting endofunctor \mathsf{L} on dcpos preserves the colimit of A_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let A_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { \left \{ A_ \infty \right \} } be a universal cocone; we must show that \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { \left \{ D \right \} } factors uniquely through \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} D to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define { \mathbf{1} _{ } } \xrightarrow{h_ \bot} { D } to be the element d_i \bot determined by an arbitrary object i \in \mathcal{I}. To see that this is well-defined, fix i,j \in \mathcal{I} to check that d_i \bot =d_j \bot. As \mathcal{I} is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag i \leadsto j \in \mathcal{I}; ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j in \mathcal{I}, we have d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot. This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone d_ \bullet.
Next we define { A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{h _{ A_ \infty }} { D } using the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }:

To arrange h _{ A_ \infty } and h_ \bot into the desired lax square, we must check that for all a_ \infty :A_ \infty we have h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } a_ \infty. Fixing arbitrary i \in \mathcal{I} and a \in A_i, it suffices to check that h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _{ i } a. As h_ \bot =d_i \bot and h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _ia = d_i \eta _{ A_i } a, our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of { \mathsf{L} {A_i} } \xrightarrow{d_i} { D }.
Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{h} { D } factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty }:

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} { D } corresponding to the cocone { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } to be the morphism h defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \mathsf{L} {A_i}, it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that h \bot = h_ \bot.
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{k,k'} { D } factoring { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } through { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } } are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } once more to reduce this to checking that k,k' agree on \bot and on \eta _{ A_ \bullet }. The former follows directly from the strictness of \mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }, as we have k \bot = k { \left ( \mathsf{L} \iota _ \bullet \bot \right )} =d_ \bullet \bot. For the latter, we must check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } =k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty }; by the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }, it suffices to check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet. By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to d_ \bullet \circ \eta _{ A_ \bullet }.

mainmatter.p: We will deduce the closure of \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } under connected colimits using general results about monads. Then we use the results of Linton to deduce the cocompleteness of \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} }.
For any dcpo A, the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \mathsf{L} takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix another lax square of the following form:
We must show that there exists a unique morphism \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D factoring the square above through { \left \{ d_ \bot \sqsubseteq d_A \right \} } as follows:

We will define \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d} D to take a ^{ ? } : \mathsf{L} {A} to the least upper bound of the directed subset U = { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_A a \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \subseteq D. As this assignment evidently has the correct factorization property, it remains just to check that it is continuous. Letting V be a directed subset of \mathsf{L} {A}, we compute:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} d \bigsqcup V & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid \bigsqcup V = \eta _A a \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid \eta _Aa \in V \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup { \left \{ \bigsqcup { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } \in V \right \} } \\ & = \bigsqcup { \left \{ da ^{ ? } \mid a ^{ ? } \in V \right \} } \end{aligned}
block.p: For uniqueness, suppose that we have two morphisms \mathsf{L} {A} \xrightarrow{d,d'} D factoring our square in the sense described above and fix a ^{ ? } : \mathsf{L} {A}:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} d a ^{ ? } & = d \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d \, { \left ( \eta _Aa \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d_Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ d' \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ d' \, { \left ( \eta _Aa \right )} \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = d' \bigsqcup \, { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup { \left \{ \eta _Aa \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} } \right )} \\ & = d'a ^{ ? } \end{aligned}

mainmatter.p: Let A_ \bullet be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \mathcal{I}. Then the lifting endofunctor \mathsf{L} on dcpos preserves the colimit of A_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let A_ \bullet \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { \left \{ A_ \infty \right \} } be a universal cocone; we must show that \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { \left \{ D \right \} } factors uniquely through \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} D to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define { \mathbf{1} _{ } } \xrightarrow{h_ \bot} { D } to be the element d_i \bot determined by an arbitrary object i \in \mathcal{I}. To see that this is well-defined, fix i,j \in \mathcal{I} to check that d_i \bot =d_j \bot. As \mathcal{I} is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag i \leadsto j \in \mathcal{I}; ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j in \mathcal{I}, we have d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot. This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone d_ \bullet.
Next we define { A_ \infty } \xrightarrow{h _{ A_ \infty }} { D } using the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }:

To arrange h _{ A_ \infty } and h_ \bot into the desired lax square, we must check that for all a_ \infty :A_ \infty we have h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } a_ \infty. Fixing arbitrary i \in \mathcal{I} and a \in A_i, it suffices to check that h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _{ i } a. As h_ \bot =d_i \bot and h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _ia = d_i \eta _{ A_i } a, our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of { \mathsf{L} {A_i} } \xrightarrow{d_i} { D }.
Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{h} { D } factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty }:

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{d_ \infty} { D } corresponding to the cocone { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } to be the morphism h defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \mathsf{L} {A_i}, it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that h \bot = h_ \bot.
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms { \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow{k,k'} { D } factoring { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{d_ \bullet} { { \left \{ D \right \} } } through { \mathsf{L} {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }} { { \left \{ \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } \right \} } } are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \mathsf{L} {A_ \infty } once more to reduce this to checking that k,k' agree on \bot and on \eta _{ A_ \bullet }. The former follows directly from the strictness of \mathsf{L} { \iota _ \bullet }, as we have k \bot = k { \left ( \mathsf{L} \iota _ \bullet \bot \right )} =d_ \bullet \bot. For the latter, we must check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } =k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty }; by the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow{\iota _ \bullet} { A_ \infty }, it suffices to check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet. By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to d_ \bullet \circ \eta _{ A_ \bullet }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} }} { \mathsf{dcpo} }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
The forgetful functor { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } \xrightarrow{\mathsf{U} ^{ \mathbb{L} }} { \mathsf{dcpo} } creates whatever colimits \mathsf{L} preserves.

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos has all small coproducts.
This is the first part of Corollary 2 of Linton's Coequalizers in categories of algebras.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The coproduct of lift-algebras is computed as a reflexive coequalizer, whose existence we have already established. Given an index set I and a diagram { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } }, the colimit of the following reflexive pair is the coproduct of { I } \xrightarrow{X _{ \bullet }} { \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } } as shown by Linton:

frontmatter.title: The category of lift-algebras is cocomplete

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb{L} = { \left ( \mathsf{L} , \eta , \mu \right )} on dcpos is cocomplete.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows because \mathsf{dcpo} ^{ \mathbb{L} } under connected colimits and coproducts
